exemplar.title: During the Vietnam War (1955 â 1975), what was the main cause that allowed the American population to understand the gravity of the experiences faced by soldiers in battle?

detailedExemplarData.title: During the Vietnam War (1955 â 1975), what was the main cause that allowed the American population to understand the gravity of the experiences faced by soldiers in battle?

strengths: The topic is communicated accurately and effectively, with clear temporal (1955â1975) and geographical (American society) scope.
The research question is focused, clearly phrased, and sustained throughout the investigation.
The student identifies two candidate factors and structures the analysis directly around the research question, demonstrating strong focus and scope definition.
weaknesses: Minor imprecision in wording (e.g. âallowedâ vs. âledâ) and occasional repetition slightly detract from full precision.
No explicit discussion of methodological rationale or criteria for source selection.
Primary sources are largely absent, and the methodology section does not address limitations or provenance.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Strand-level assessments placed this work solidly in the 5-6 band for both topic communication and research question focus. Methodology, while adequate in range, lacks explicit reflection on source selection and omits primary material, placing it in the 3-4 band (score 4). On balance, the studentâs work merits a strong performance in Focus and Method, but the absence of methodological justification prevents the full 6. A score of 5 reflects the overall strength and minor deficits.

strengths: The introduction provides clear chronological and geopolitical context, demonstrating sound subject knowledge.
Relevant historical terminology (e.g. âguerilla warfare,â âTet Offensive,â âpropagandaâ) is used accurately in most instances.
Quantitative data from peer-reviewed studies (e.g. American Journal of Public Health) is integrated to deepen understanding of drug use prevalence.
weaknesses: Frequent reliance on tertiary and popular sources (Britannica Kids, History.com, blogs) undermines scholarly rigour.
Occasional factual imprecision and overgeneralisation (e.g. casualty figures, television reach).
Terminology and concepts are not consistently precise, with some misleading or awkward usages (e.g. âsensoringâ).
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The essay demonstrates clear basic knowledge and mostly appropriate use of sources, but treatment remains largely descriptive with uneven scholarly depth. Strand assessments uniformly fell in the 3-4 band and lean toward the lower end due to imprecision and overreliance on non-scholarly sources. A score of 3 accurately reflects this mid-band performance.

strengths: The investigation introduces and balances conflicting scholarly perspectives (e.g. Kratz vs. Mandelbaum), showing an attempt to weigh evidence.
Analytical links between graphic television imagery and anti-war protest movements are articulated cogently.
Inclusion of counter-arguments (e.g. KamiÅskiâs critique of media sensationalism) adds nuance to the discussion.
weaknesses: Key primary sources (original broadcasts, veteransâ testimonies, contemporaneous polls) are absent, limiting research sophistication.
Evaluation of source reliability and bias is only briefly noted and not systematically explored.
Some conclusions overreach the evidence provided, and the final synthesis lacks depth and completeness.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Research quality aligns with the 4-6 band but is weakened by the absence of primary evidence. Analysis and evaluation similarly fall in the lower half of the 4-6 band, with partial support and superficial treatment of limitations. An overall Critical Thinking score of 4 reflects a coherent but only moderately supported argument.

strengths: All required structural elements are present (title page, table of contents, labeled sections, conclusion, works cited).
Clear headings and sub-headings facilitate logical progression and reader navigation.
The detailed table of contents provides an effective roadmap of the investigationâs structure.
weaknesses: Citation and formatting inconsistencies (malformed URLs, duplicated entries, errant commas) detract from polish.
Key title-page elements (abstract, full candidate details) are missing or placeholder headers remain.
Redundant headers break immersion and minor typographical errors persist.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The workâs structure is clearly appropriate (band 3-4), but inconsistent adherence to citation style and missing title-page details lower its overall presentation quality. A Presentation score of 3 reflects effective organization tempered by formatting and completeness issues.

strandSelections.reasoning:
The layout generally follows academic conventions: consistent font, section headings, pagination (as indicated in the table of contents), and an attempt at MLA citation style. The work is within the word limit and supplies a word count.  

However, citation formatting is inconsistent, some URLs are malformed, and there are minor typographical errors (double commas, mismatched parentheses). The works cited list contains duplicated or truncated entries, signalling imperfect adherence to style guidelines.  

These shortcomings place the work within, but not at the top of, the 3-4 band for layout and formatting; a score of 3 is appropriate.

The conclusion reiterates the central argument that television was the principal cause influencing public understanding and attempts to synthesise evidence from both thematic sections. There is some acknowledgement of limitations (e.g., untested soldiers, media exaggeration).  

Yet evaluation remains partial: reliability and bias of sources are noted only briefly, and alternative explanations (e.g., political activism, casualty figures) are not systematically weighed. The line of reasoning is generally coherent but occasionally repetitive and lacks depth.  

These characteristics fit the 4-6 band for discussion and evaluation, with modest critical engagement warranting a score of 4.

The student states the topic explicitly in the title, introduction and table of contents, using clear time parameters (1955-1975) and specifying both the geographical focus (American society) and the phenomenon under investigation (understanding soldiersâ battle experiences).  

The explanation of why the topic is worthy of study is sufficiently developed: the introduction stresses the Vietnam Warâs social, psychological and political impact and links this to changing public perceptions.  

Minor issues of wording (e.g. occasional repetition and clumsy phrasing) marginally affect precision, so a top-of-band score is not fully warranted; nevertheless the topic is communicated accurately and is entirely appropriate for a History EE, justifying a strong band 5 performance.

The essay contains all required structural elements: title page with word count, abstract omitted (as per current EE guidelines), table of contents, introduction, clearly labelled sections, conclusion and works cited. Headings and sub-headings facilitate logical flow, and the argument broadly progresses from context to analysis to conclusion.  

Although some sections are unevenly balanced and there is occasional redundancy, the overall organisation is effective and supports reader understanding. Therefore, it comfortably meets the descriptor for the 3-4 band, meriting the top score of 4.

The student employs a reasonable range of secondary sources (academic articles, government blogs, popular history sites) and structures them into thematic sub-sections. Source selection is generally relevant to the research question, with contrasting viewpoints (e.g., Kratz vs. Mandelbaum).  

Nonetheless, the methodology is only partially explicit.  There is no discussion of criteria for source selection, provenance, or limitations, and primary sources (e.g., contemporary broadcasts, veteransâ testimony) are largely absent.  Citations include non-scholarly outlets (Britannica Kids, History.com, blogs), which weakens the appropriateness of the source set.  

Given the adequate but uneven completeness and limited evidence of informed selection, the work merits the upper end of the 3-4 band, i.e., a score of 4.

The investigation draws on a moderate range of sources, including government archives, academic journals, and contemporary commentators, and most are pertinent to the stated question. Conflicting interpretations (e.g., Kratz vs. Mandelbaum) are introduced, showing an attempt to balance perspectives.  

Nonetheless, key primary sources such as original broadcasts, veteran testimonies, or contemporaneous polls are absent, and some cited materials are tertiary or popular in nature. This limits the overall sophistication and breadth of the research base.  

Consequently, the strand aligns with the 4-6 band for Critical Thinking; the generally relevant but not consistently rigorous research quality earns a mid-band score of 5.

The essay moves beyond description by comparing two causal factors and weighing their relative significance, incorporating some statistical data and scholarly debate. These elements demonstrate attempts at analysis.  

However, the reasoning is frequently assertive rather than evidential; large claims about televisionâs impact are made without systematic linkage to primary data, and counter-arguments receive limited, surface-level treatment. Conclusions sometimes over-reach the evidence provided.  

Given partially supported analysis with noticeable gaps, the work meets but does not exceed the 4-6 band standard; a score of 4 reflects the lower end of that range.

Most cited materials relate directly to the research question (television coverage, drug use statistics, public opinion). The student integrates quotations and data to illustrate key points, indicating an understanding of source relevance.  

However, reliance on tertiary or popular sources (Britannica Kids, History.com, blog posts) reduces scholarly rigour, and primary evidence (e.g., news footage, contemporaneous polls) is largely missing. Source material is often summarised rather than critically deployed to support nuanced argumentation.  

This places the work solidly in the 3-4 band but closer to the lower end, justifying a score of 3 for application of source material.

The essay shows a competent grasp of the Vietnam Warâs chronology, key events (e.g., Tet Offensive, My Lai), and social context. The student explains the concepts of media censorship, public opinion, and drug abuse effects with reasonable clarity, moving beyond mere narrative in places.  

Yet the treatment is often descriptive and contains occasional factual imprecision (e.g., conflating casualty figures, over-generalising televisionâs reach). Depth of historiographical understanding is limited; scholarly debates are touched on but not explored in detail.  

Overall, knowledge is clear but not consistently analytical or nuanced, aligning with the middle 3-4 band and warranting a score of 3.

The research questionââDuring the Vietnam War (1955â1975), what was the main cause that allowed the American population to understand the gravity of the experiences faced by soldiers in battle?ââis clearly phrased, focused on a single causal relationship and anchored in time and place.  

Throughout the essay the student consistently returns to this question, organising the analysis around two candidate causes (television coverage and drug/alcohol abuse) and weighing their relative significance. This demonstrates sustained focus.  

However, the phrasing remains somewhat awkward ("allowed" rather than "led"; duplication of wording), and the concept of âmain causeâ is acknowledged but not precisely operationalised, so a mid-high score of 5 best reflects the quality rather than the full 6.

The student employs relevant historical terms such as "guerilla warfare," "Tet Offensive," and "propaganda," and uses concepts like media censorship and public perception accurately in most cases.  

However, terminology is not always preciseâe.g., referring to South Vietnamese forces as âCatholic communist oppositionistsâ is misleading, and phrases such as âsensoringâ (sic) or awkward repetitions detract from accuracy. Conceptual discussion of causation and historiography is only partial.  

Given generally correct but inconsistent use of subject-specific language and concepts, performance fits the 3-4 band; a score of 3 is appropriate.

annotations.comment: Grammar error ârate of television were very greatâ disrupts professional tone; revise for subject-verb agreement and clarity.
Reference to âDemocratic Senator Thomas J.â is incomplete and ambiguous; provide full name and context to clarify authority.
The subsection heading signals the shift to analyzing drug abuse impacts clearly, supporting the essayâs logical progression.
The subsection question on television as âbackground noiseâ succinctly frames a contrasting scholarly viewpoint.
Acknowledgment of testing limitations is good but would benefit from further discussion of selection bias and data reliability.
Janosâs retrospective narrative is adopted uncritically; evaluate how contemporary context might influence his interpretation of events.
Several TOC entries lack page numbers (e.g. "2: Analysis" and its subâheadings), which impedes navigation; ensure every entry lists its page.
Analysis heavily draws on a tertiary source; deepen the discussion by integrating primary footage transcripts or veteransâ testimony.
The phrasing âallowed the American populationâ is vague; consider rewording to âled the American publicâ and operationalising âmain causeâ with clear criteria.
The detailed table of contents provides a coherent roadmap of sections and subsections, facilitating reader navigation through the investigation.
Section 2.1 is clearly labeled and distinguished, aiding the readerâs understanding of the analytical structure and thematic breakdown.
The link between graphic television imagery and the emergence of protest movements is articulated cogently, demonstrating effective analytical reasoning.
A redundant research question header appears at top of each page, breaking immersion; remove repeated headers in body pages.
No methodology section explains rationale for source selection or why primary sources were excluded; add explicit methodological reflection.
The placeholder header âTab 1â offers no academic context; replace it with a proper title page including investigation title, candidate name, and abstract as per EE guidelines.
This page lacks essential information such as author details, abstract, and investigation statement; ensure all required elements appear on the title page.
The research question is clearly stated at the outset and anchored in time (1955â1975) and place (American society), demonstrating strong focus and scope definition.
Introduction offers clear chronological and geopolitical context, demonstrating sound subject knowledge and setting up the investigation effectively.
Typographical error âpsychologically,,â disrupts readability; conduct a thorough proofread to correct punctuation and improve precision.
The student clearly identifies and frames two candidate factors, establishing a focused analytical structure tied directly to the research question.
Conclusion ends abruptly midâidea and repeats points; strengthen by restating the research question and offering a concise synthesis of evidence.
Frequent reliance on a single blog source (Kratz) limits scholarly depth; incorporate primary accounts or peerâreviewed articles to strengthen evidence.
The subsection title is excessively verbose; streamline language to maintain reader focus and improve concision.
Major Mitchellâs survey is cited without details on sample size or methodology; specify these to assess the robustness of his conclusions.
Works cited formatting is inconsistent (missing italics, malformed URLs); apply a single citation style consistently throughout.
Integration of quantitative data from the American Journal of Public Health study deepens the readerâs understanding of drug use prevalence.
The heading on media exploitation offers clear transition to the final thematic perspective, maintaining structural coherence.
The conclusion section is clearly labeled, helping readers identify the summative discussion of findings.
Incorporating KamiÅskiâs counterâargument enriches the discussion by presenting a nuanced critique of media sensationalism.
Footnote markers interrupt paragraph flow; reposition them at sentence end to improve readability.
